hypothesis: After repeated failures from residual/gating variants, relaxing the per-sample hard support constraint may recover a better mid-K tradeoff: `batch_topk` at `K=24, EF=12` could improve reconstruction efficiency over fixed-sample top-k and challenge the current `K=24` frontier point without increasing K or EF.
summary: Run a pure architecture comparison at `K=24, EF=12` with `batch_topk` under the pinned recipe `OPTIMIZER=signum`, `LR=3.2e-3`, `AUXK_ALPHA=0.0`. This follows the severe-stagnation policy by trying a fundamentally different sparsity mechanism instead of any further scalar sweep.
self_review: I avoided LR, aux, K, and EF tweaks because ten no-improve rounds means minor tuning is exhausted. I also kept the comparison at K=24 so the readout stays directly comparable to the current frontier slot while testing a genuinely different support-allocation mechanism.
notes_to_memory: Round 45 continues severe-stagnation recovery with another fundamentally different built-in family after `multi_branch_gated`, `jumprelu`, and `two_stage_residual` all failed badly at K=24.
Keep the run architecture-only at K=24, EF=12 with all recipe fields explicitly pinned.
`batch_topk` tests whether a looser support-budget allocation works better than strict per-sample top-k in the frontier-relevant mid-K regime.
next_hypotheses: If `batch_topk` is competitive but slightly worse at K=24, test K=16 to see whether flexible budget allocation helps more at tighter sparsity.
If `batch_topk` clearly loses at K=24, reject this support-allocation branch and move to another fundamentally different family such as `residual_vq` rather than any scalar sweep.
If `batch_topk` wins at K=24, follow with K=16 or K=32 to map whether the gain generalizes across the mid-K band.
